stem: A hospital uses a TSH blood test to screen for hypothyroidism. The test has a sensitivity of 91% and a specificity of 94%. The prevalence of hypothyroidism in the patient population is estimated to be between 2.7% and 5.2%, with uncertainty due to regional variation and demographic differences in the hospital's catchment area. A patient receives a positive test result. What is the probability that the patient actually has hypothyroidism? Provide your answer as a probability with a confidence interval reflecting the uncertainty in prevalence.

ground_truth.answer: P(hypothyroidism | positive test) ranges from 0.296 to 0.454 as prevalence varies from 2.7% to 5.2%. Point estimate at 4.0% prevalence: 0.387.

solution_steps: Apply Bayes' theorem: P(D|+) = P(+|D)×P(D) / [P(+|D)×P(D) + P(+|¬D)×P(¬D)], where P(+|¬D) = 1-specificity = 0.06.
The confidence interval [0.296, 0.454] reflects the range of posterior probabilities over plausible prevalence values.
common_failure_modes: Confusing sensitivity (91%) with positive predictive value
Providing only a point estimate without acknowledging the prevalence uncertainty
Ignoring the base rate (prevalence) and using the test sensitivity as the answer
